posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
Some weekend wines:

2014 Domaine Denis Mortet, Gevrey-Chambertin, Mes Cinq Terroirs
Mortet is a bit of a Dangerfield Burg to me. The house is never top of mind for me, but when I have one I’m reminded how just right-down-the-middle delicious their wines can be. This particular bottle was wide open and reflective of the village and vintage. It strikes me as a good “group” wine that would appeal to geeks and hoi polloi alike. A mix of fragrant strawberry and boysenberry fruit, with decent cut and acidity. It’s fleshy and has an alluring nose. Not a lot of nuances … just straight-ahead jazz. It did fade on day 2 … I believe this is an assemblage of various lieux dits that might have been bottled separately in past.
2007 Hubert Lignier, Morey St. Denis, 1er Cru, Vieilles Vignes
I’ve noted before that this is one of the all-time relative QPR wines for me – a Clos de la Roche in sheep’s clothing. But this particular bottle, while attractive and satisfying, lacked some of the drive and tannic grip I associate with this wine. Truly a product of the vintage – open-knit, transparent but just a little bit of a bantamweight. Don’t get me wrong – there’s the Lignier spice and loam and the fruit is ripe and delicious. I just wanted a bit more rockiness and heft. It is more Chambolle than Morey.

2011 Produttori del Barbaresco, Barbaresco Normale
From magnum, this wine totally caught me off guard. It looks and smells like a typical Produttori, but the palate seems so soft. It’s very easy and round-- and was a crowd pleaser at Sunday dinner with non-obsessives. It’s rich and medium-bodied, but the wine came off a bit soft and squishy to me. I’m surprised to write that because usually I wish the crus were just a bit more giving and engaging. But I missed those puckering tannins and the diffident mouthfeel. This was all a bit too easy for me. Another product of a hotter vintage?

But this particular bottle, while attractive and satisfying, lacked some of the drive and tannic grip I associate with this wine. I’m surprised to write that because usually I wish the crus were just a bit more giving and engaging. But I missed those puckering tannins and the diffident mouthfeel. This was all a bit too easy for me. Another product of a hotter vintage?

The Mortet bottling has been made for years, I forget the exact vineyard make up but it’s been the same since at least 03’…

posts.name: David Cooper
posts.cooked: Thanks for the note on the 2011 Produttori. I have had it 5 times now and find it very approachable. I think it’s interesting how we laud traditional non interventalist producers, but then when the vintage gives us a less serious wine people are surprised.

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the note on the 2011 Produttori. I have had it 5 times now and find it very approachable. I think it’s interesting how we laud traditional non interventalist producers, but then when the vintage gives us a less serious wine people are surprised.

David, you are right. I know objectively I shouldn’t have been surprised. It was just the first time I had such an easy-going wine from Produttori. I usually feel like I have to gird for battle when opening a Produttori bottle relatively early in its lifecycle.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The Mortet bottling has been made for years, I forget the exact vineyard make up but it’s been the same since at least 03’…

Not so sure about that. I think Arnaud is now using the label for the blend of all his Gevrey AOC lieux-dits. I need to ask him about that but he is in full blown vilification right now so it will have to wait. Either way, a delicious wine, and one that’s especially good in 2015 if you see it.

Arnaud’s dad’s allegedly too oaky renditions from vintages such as 2005 are beginning to drink beautifully today, by the way. Wines that really did come good in the end, unlike so many.

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked: Full on vilification must be taxing. Not too mention the fulmination!

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the note on the 2011 Produttori. I have had it 5 times now and find it very approachable. I think it’s interesting how we laud traditional non interventalist producers, but then when the vintage gives us a less serious wine people are surprised.

David, you are right. I know objectively I shouldn’t have been surprised. It was just the first time I had such an easy-going wine from Produttori. I usually feel like I have to gird for battle when opening a Produttori bottle relatively early in its lifecycle.

When I was at the Produttori in June , I had the chance to taste 8 vintages of the Ovello Riserva. Not that is not the regular Barbaresco, but the vintage variation was obvious. There were the tannic vintages ('04, '08, '13), the middle of the road vintages ('05, '07!, '14) and the couple of insanely approachable vintages ('09 - which already seemed old, '11). The wines really were a glimpse into both terroir and vintage. I treasure that experience, as it taught me more about loving the variations than any other tasting I have done.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
David
Don’t encourage Aldo, or else he’ll start selling 9 (vineyard) by 9 (vintage) tasting packs!
Regards
Ian

posts.name: craig v
posts.cooked:
I’ve had the 2015 Mortet and loved its purity and balance. Far and away best 2015 for me so far. I’d love to try the 2014 or a 2010

posts.name: Tom Maskell
posts.cooked:
Matt,

I’ve had 10-12 Produttori 2011 Riservas since release. None have been shut down or overly tannic. In fact after several early tastings, and on the advice of a couple of Nebbheads, I bought them because they are unlikely to shut down, and have an appealing fruit forward profile. And they are much better than the 2009s. Your experience is not surprising.

Regards

posts.cooked:
Thanks for the note on the 2011 Produttori. I have had it 5 times now and find it very approachable. I think it’s interesting how we laud traditional non interventalist producers, but then when the vintage gives us a less serious wine people are surprised.

I know what you mean. I still have a magnum of the 99 and previous bottles seemed decades away.

posts.name: Claus Jeppesen
posts.cooked:
I’ve had the 2015 Mortet and loved its purity and balance. Far and away best 2015 for me so far. I’d love to try the 2014 or a 2010

Some weekend wines:

2014 Domaine Denis Mortet, Gevrey-Chambertin, Mes Cinq Terroirs
Mortet is a bit of a Dangerfield Burg to me. The house is never top of mind for me, but when I have one I’m reminded how just right-down-the-middle delicious their wines can be. This particular bottle was wide open and reflective of the village and vintage. It strikes me as a good “group” wine that would appeal to geeks and hoi polloi alike. A mix of fragrant strawberry and boysenberry fruit, with decent cut and acidity. It’s fleshy and has an alluring nose. Not a lot of nuances … just straight-ahead jazz. It did fade on day 2 … I believe this is an assemblage of various lieux dits that might have been bottled separately in past.
2007 Hubert Lignier, Morey St. Denis, 1er Cru, Vieilles Vignes
I’ve noted before that this is one of the all-time relative QPR wines for me – a Clos de la Roche in sheep’s clothing. But this particular bottle, while attractive and satisfying, lacked some of the drive and tannic grip I associate with this wine. Truly a product of the vintage – open-knit, transparent but just a little bit of a bantamweight. Don’t get me wrong – there’s the Lignier spice and loam and the fruit is ripe and delicious. I just wanted a bit more rockiness and heft. It is more Chambolle than Morey.

2011 Produttori del Barbaresco, Barbaresco Normale
From magnum, this wine totally caught me off guard. It looks and smells like a typical Produttori, but the palate seems so soft. It’s very easy and round-- and was a crowd pleaser at Sunday dinner with non-obsessives. It’s rich and medium-bodied, but the wine came off a bit soft and squishy to me. I’m surprised to write that because usually I wish the crus were just a bit more giving and engaging. But I missed those puckering tannins and the diffident mouthfeel. This was all a bit too easy for me. Another product of a hotter vintage?

The Mortet bottling has been made for years, I forget the exact vineyard make up but it’s been the same since at least 03’…

Between 2006 and 2012 Arnoud used the material from En Motrot, Au Vellé, En Champs, La Combe du Dessus and En Dérée in the Vielles Vignes bottling, I believe.
Mes Cinq Terroirs 2005 and VV 2006, 2007, 2009 and 2010 are remarkable wines
Thanks for the notes

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the note on the 2011 Produttori. I have had it 5 times now and find it very approachable. I think it’s interesting how we laud traditional non interventalist producers, but then when the vintage gives us a less serious wine people are surprised.

David, you are right. I know objectively I shouldn’t have been surprised. It was just the first time I had such an easy-going wine from Produttori. I usually feel like I have to gird for battle when opening a Produttori bottle relatively early in its lifecycle.

I wonder if riper, softer lots went into the normale bottling. There were a lot of '11 Barolos and Barbarescos that were a bit soft and diffuse for my tastes. But when a group of mine tasted through all nine crus in 2011 two years ago (notes here), they were not that way. They showed really well, with good structure. The normale sounds like it might reflect the vintage more than the crus.

link_counts.title: TN: Side-by-side tasting of all nine Produttori crus in 2011 - Wine Berserkers - international wine social media, online community, and discussion

posts.cooked:
I’ve had the 2015 Mortet and loved its purity and balance. Far and away best 2015 for me so far. I’d love to try the 2014 or a 2010

The Mortet bottling has been made for years, I forget the exact vineyard make up but it’s been the same since at least 03’…

Between 2006 and 2012 Arnoud used the material from En Motrot, Au Vellé, En Champs, La Combe du Dessus and En Dérée in the Vielles Vignes bottling, I believe.
Mes Cinq Terroirs 2005 and VV 2006, 2007, 2009 and 2010 are remarkable wines
Thanks for the notes

Claus, thanks for that, do you know what vineyards were used before and after?

posts.name: Claus Jeppesen
posts.cooked:
Mes Cinq Terroirs was En Motrot, Au Vellé, En Champs, La Combe du Dessus and En Dérée
Gevrey-Chambertin VV was Combe du Dessus and En Derée
Gevrey-Chambertin was En Motrot and Au Vellé
I do not know the data after 2012

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
The normale sounds like it might reflect the vintage more than the crus.

Perhaps it shows the extremes of the vintage more than the crus. The crus are still softer than more classic vintages such as 2008 or 2013.

It’s also worth noting that the regular Barbaresco typically contains (per Aldo Vacca) more Ovello and Pora than any of the other sites, due to their larger holdings. Pora is pretty much always one of the “friendlier” wines, while Ovello is more middle of the pack, but certainly not in the top tier of tannic crus from the Produttori.
